questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between Ridge (L2) and Lasso (L1) regularization in terms of what each does to model coefficients?
options: Ridge removes features with low variance; Lasso removes features with high correlation.

Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never exactly to zero — it distributes the penalty proportionally. Lasso can shrink coefficients exactly to zero, effectively performing automatic feature selection by eliminating unimportant features from the model entirely.
Ridge uses the absolute value of coefficients; Lasso uses squared coefficients.
Ridge is for regression problems; Lasso is only for classification problems.
questions.answer: Ridge shrinks all coefficients toward zero but never exactly to zero — it distributes the penalty proportionally. Lasso can shrink coefficients exactly to zero, effectively performing automatic feature selection by eliminating unimportant features from the model entirely.
questions.explanation: Given 1000 features where 50 are truly predictive: Ridge uses all 1000 but with small coefficients. Lasso zeroes out the 950 irrelevant ones, producing a sparse, interpretable model with only 50 non-zero coefficients.

questions.question: A Logistic Regression model has a coefficient of +2.3 for the feature 'support_calls'. What exactly does this coefficient represent?
options: For each additional support call, the churn probability increases by 2.3%.

For each unit increase in support_calls, the log-odds of the positive class (churn) increases by 2.3 — meaning the odds of churning multiply by e^2.3 ≈ 10x. Logistic Regression coefficients represent changes in log-odds, not direct probability changes.
The model is 2.3 times more confident in its predictions for this feature.
A coefficient of 2.3 means support_calls is the 2nd or 3rd most important feature.
questions.answer: For each unit increase in support_calls, the log-odds of the positive class (churn) increases by 2.3 — meaning the odds of churning multiply by e^2.3 ≈ 10x. Logistic Regression coefficients represent changes in log-odds, not direct probability changes.
questions.explanation: This interpretability is why Logistic Regression is preferred in regulated industries. A bank must explain to regulators WHY a loan was denied. 'Support calls increase churn odds by 10x' is a clear, auditable explanation.

questions.question: A model has very low training error but very high test error. According to the bias-variance tradeoff, what is the diagnosis and the appropriate fix?
options: High bias (underfitting) — the model is too simple. Fix: add more features or complexity.

High variance (overfitting) — the model has memorized training data including noise. Low train error + high test error is the classic signature. Fixes: more training data, stronger regularization, simpler model (fewer parameters/depth), or dropout for neural networks.
The training and test data come from different distributions (dataset shift).
The evaluation metric is inappropriate for this type of problem.
questions.answer: High variance (overfitting) — the model has memorized training data including noise. Low train error + high test error is the classic signature. Fixes: more training data, stronger regularization, simpler model (fewer parameters/depth), or dropout for neural networks.
questions.explanation: The diagnosis is always: train error high → underfitting (high bias). Train error low, test error high → overfitting (high variance). Train error low, test error low → good generalization. Train error high in both → new pattern: possible dataset quality issue.
